question_text: A substance has a melting point of 100°C and a boiling point of 300°C. Starting from a solid at 20°C, the substance is slowly heated. Describe all the state changes that occur and explain what happens at the molecular level during each change, including the role of heat energy.
correct_answer: From 20°C to 100°C: solid particles vibrate more as temperature increases (no state change). At 100°C: melting occurs - particles gain enough energy to overcome attractive forces and move freely (liquid). From 100°C to 300°C: temperature increases, particles move faster in the liquid. At 300°C: boiling occurs - all particles throughout the liquid gain enough energy to escape as a gas. The substance becomes a gas. Throughout, heat energy increases particle kinetic energy and eventually breaks the bonds holding particles in fixed positions.
explanation: Different state changes occur at different temperatures because particles need specific amounts of energy to overcome the forces holding them in place. Heating provides this energy, first increasing molecular motion, then causing state changes when threshold energies are reached.
